subject: Physics
q: A block moving horizontally on a smooth surface with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> splits into two equal parts. If one of the parts moves at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the same direction, then the fractional change in the kinetic energy will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ________.
question: A block moving horizontally on a smooth surface with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> splits into two equal parts. If one of the parts moves at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the same direction, then the fractional change in the kinetic energy will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ________.
solution:
2m×40=60×m+mv
V=20 m/s
Now ΔKE=KEf-KEi=12 m×(20)2+12 m×(60)2-122 m×(40)2
ΔKE=12 m(800)⇒KEi=122 m×(40)2=12 m×3200
Fractional change =ΔKEKEi=14

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statements is false for a particle moving in a circle with a constant angular speed?
question: Which of the following statements is false for a particle moving in a circle with a constant angular speed?
options: The velocity vector is tangent to the circle.
The acceleration vector is tangent to the circle.
The acceleration vector points to the centre of the circle.
The velocity and acceleration vectors are perpendicular to each other.
solution: The acceleration vector is along the radius of circle.

subject: Physics
q: A spring of spring constant $5 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is stretched initially by $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the unstretched position. Then the work required to stretch it further by another $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ is
question: A spring of spring constant $5 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is stretched initially by $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the unstretched position. Then the work required to stretch it further by another $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ is
solution:
Required work done
$=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~K}\left(\mathrm{x}_2^2-\mathrm{x}_1^2\right)=\frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 10^3\left[10^2-5^2\right] \times 10^{-4}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 75 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-4}=18.75$

subject: Physics
q:
A rolling wheel of 12 kg is on an inclined plane at position P and connected to a mass of 3 kg through a string of fixed length and pulley as shown in figure.
Consider PR as friction free surface.
The velocity of centre of mass of the wheel when it reaches at the bottom Q of the inclined plane PQ will be 12xgh m s-1. The value of x (rounded off to the nearest integer) is _____.
question:
A rolling wheel of 12 kg is on an inclined plane at position P and connected to a mass of 3 kg through a string of fixed length and pulley as shown in figure.
Consider PR as friction free surface.
The velocity of centre of mass of the wheel when it reaches at the bottom Q of the inclined plane PQ will be 12xgh m s-1. The value of x (rounded off to the nearest integer) is _____.
solution:
If we take the wheel as a ring then its moment of inertia will be, I=Mr2.

Using mechanical energy conservation,

Mgh-mgh=12mv2+12Mv2+12Iω2⇒12gh-3gh=123v2+1212v2+1212r2v2r2

⇒9gh=15v2+12v22

⇒v=23gh=1283gh

Therefore, x=83≃3

subject: Physics
q: A particle gets displaced by $\Delta \bar{r}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \mathrm{m}$ under the action of a force $\vec{F}=(7 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$. The change in its kinetic energy is
question: A particle gets displaced by $\Delta \bar{r}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \mathrm{m}$ under the action of a force $\vec{F}=(7 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$. The change in its kinetic energy is
solution: According to work-energy theorem, Change in kinetic energy $=$ work done $=\vec{F} \cdot \Delta \vec{r}=(7 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})$ $=14+12+12=38 \mathrm{~J}$

question: A spring of unstretched length 1 has a mass $m$ with one end fixed to a rigid support. Assuming spring to be made of a uniform wire, the kinetic energy possessed by it if its free end is pulled with uniform velocity $v$ is:
solution: No Solution Available

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass 1 kg is pushed up a surface inclined to horizontal at an angle of 60° by a force of 10 N parallel to the inclined surface as shown in figure. When the block is pushed up by 10 m along inclined surface, the work done against frictional force is : g=10 m s-2
question: A block of mass 1 kg is pushed up a surface inclined to horizontal at an angle of 60° by a force of 10 N parallel to the inclined surface as shown in figure. When the block is pushed up by 10 m along inclined surface, the work done against frictional force is : g=10 m s-2
solution:
Work done against frictional force  

=μN×10=μmgcos60°×10

=0.1×5×10=5 J

subject: Physics
q: Arrange the four graphs in descending order of total work done; where W1,W2,W3 and W4 are the work done corresponding to figure a,b,c and d respectively.
question: Arrange the four graphs in descending order of total work done; where W1,W2,W3 and W4 are the work done corresponding to figure a,b,c and d respectively.
solution:
As seen from figure, work done in first graph, Wa=0.

Now, work done in figure b, Wb=+ve, and that in figure c, Wc=+ve, >Wb.

In last graph, work done is Wd=-ve.

So, the descending order of total work done is 

⇒Wc>Wb>Wa>Wd

⇒W3>W2>W1>W4

subject: Physics
q: A uniform chain of length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is kept on a table such that a length of $60 \mathrm{~cm}$ hangs freely from the edge of the table. The total mass of the chain is $4 \mathrm{~kg}$. What is the work done in pulling the entire chain on the table?
question: A uniform chain of length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is kept on a table such that a length of $60 \mathrm{~cm}$ hangs freely from the edge of the table. The total mass of the chain is $4 \mathrm{~kg}$. What is the work done in pulling the entire chain on the table?
solution: Work done $=\mathrm{mgh}=1.2 \times 0.3 \times 10=3.6 \mathrm{~J}$

subject: Physics
q: An annular ring with inner and outer radii $R_1$ and $R_2$ is rolling without slipping with a uniform angular speed. The ratio of the forces experienced by the two particles situated on the inner and outer parts of the ring, $F_1 / F_2$ is
question: An annular ring with inner and outer radii $R_1$ and $R_2$ is rolling without slipping with a uniform angular speed. The ratio of the forces experienced by the two particles situated on the inner and outer parts of the ring, $F_1 / F_2$ is
solution: $\frac{\mathrm{F}_1}{\mathrm{~F}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_1 \omega^2}{\mathrm{R}_2 \omega^2}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_1}{\mathrm{R}_2}$

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving in a medium and experiences a frictional force <math> <mrow> <mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>−</mo><mi>k</mi><msup> <mi>v</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>.</mo></mrow> </math> Its initial speed is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> its kinetic energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msubsup><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn><mn>2</mn></msubsup><mo>,</mo></math> then value of <math> <mi>k</mi> </math> will be:-
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving in a medium and experiences a frictional force <math> <mrow> <mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>−</mo><mi>k</mi><msup> <mi>v</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>.</mo></mrow> </math> Its initial speed is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> its kinetic energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msubsup><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn><mn>2</mn></msubsup><mo>,</mo></math> then value of <math> <mi>k</mi> </math> will be:-
solution:
After 10 sec, 12mv2=18mv02
⇒v=v02=5 m s-1
Acceleration,  a=− k v 2 m
⇒  dv dt =− k v 2 m
⇒ ∫105dvv2=−km ∫010dt
-15+110=-k10-2×10
k=10−4 kg m-1

question: The velocity-time graph of a particle of mass 10 kg is shown in the figure. The net work done on the particle in the first two seconds of the motion is

subject: Physics
q: A pendulum is suspended by a string of length 250 cm. The mass of the bob of the pendulum is 200 g. The bob is pulled aside until the string is at 60° with vertical as shown in the figure. After releasing the bob, the maximum velocity attained by the bob will be _____ m s-1. (if g=10 m s-2)
question: A pendulum is suspended by a string of length 250 cm. The mass of the bob of the pendulum is 200 g. The bob is pulled aside until the string is at 60° with vertical as shown in the figure. After releasing the bob, the maximum velocity attained by the bob will be _____ m s-1. (if g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Velocity will be maximum at the bottom point when potential energy of the pendulum will be zero(considering bottom point as reference for potential energy).

12mv2=mgl-lcos60°v=gl=10×2.5=5 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A particle m=1kg slides down a frictionless track AOC starting from rest at a point A (height 2m ). After reaching C, the particle continues to move freely in air as a projectile. When it reaching its highest point P (height 1m ), the kinetic energy of the particle (in J ) is: (Figure drawn is schematic and not to scale; take g=10ms-2 ) ______________.
question: A particle m=1kg slides down a frictionless track AOC starting from rest at a point A (height 2m ). After reaching C, the particle continues to move freely in air as a projectile. When it reaching its highest point P (height 1m ), the kinetic energy of the particle (in J ) is: (Figure drawn is schematic and not to scale; take g=10ms-2 ) ______________.
solution:
KEP-KEA=PEP-PEA=mghP-mghA
=1×10×2-1×10×1=10J

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves in one dimension from rest under the influence of a force that varies with the distance traveled by the particle as shown in the figure. The kinetic energy of the particle after it has traveled 3 m is:
question: A particle moves in one dimension from rest under the influence of a force that varies with the distance traveled by the particle as shown in the figure. The kinetic energy of the particle after it has traveled 3 m is:
solution:
Change in K.E.=Work done=Area
Area=(3×2)+(12×1×1)=6.5
∴ Change in kinetic energy=6.5 J

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving in a circular path of radius a under the action of an attractive potential <math><mi>U</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> Its total energy is:
question: A particle is moving in a circular path of radius a under the action of an attractive potential <math><mi>U</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> Its total energy is:
solution:
U=-K2r2⇒F=-dUdr=2K2r3=Kr3⇒mv2r=Kr3⇒v2=Kmr2
⇒12mv2=K2r2⇒E=K2r2-K2r2=0.

subject: Physics
q: A bullet of mass 200 g having initial kinetic energy 90 J is shot inside a long swimming pool as shown in the figure. If it's kinetic energy reduces to 40 J within 1 s, the minimum length of the pool, the bullet has to travel so that it completely comes to rest is
question: A bullet of mass 200 g having initial kinetic energy 90 J is shot inside a long swimming pool as shown in the figure. If it's kinetic energy reduces to 40 J within 1 s, the minimum length of the pool, the bullet has to travel so that it completely comes to rest is
solution:
Let us assume that the motion of the bullet is of constant retardation inside the water.

Initial kinetic energy 12mv02=90 J

⇒v0=30 m s-1

Let the retardation is a, then after 1 s

12m30-a×12=40 J

⇒30-a=20

⇒a=10 m s-2

Stopping distance for the constant retardation is given by,

l=u22a=30220=45 m.

Therefore, the minimum length of the pool required is 45 m.

subject: Physics
q: The rain drop of mass $1$ g, starts with zero velocity from a height of $1$ km. It hits the ground with a speed of $5$ m/s. The work done by the unknown resistive force is _______ J. (take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: The rain drop of mass $1$ g, starts with zero velocity from a height of $1$ km. It hits the ground with a speed of $5$ m/s. The work done by the unknown resistive force is _______ J. (take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Using the work-energy theorem, the total work done by all forces equals the change in kinetic energy:

$W_{g} + W_{r} = \Delta K$

$mgh + W_{r} = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 - 0$

Substituting the given values ($m = 10^{-3}$ kg, $g = 10$ m/s$^2$, $h = 1000$ m, $v = 5$ m/s):

$(10^{-3})(10)(1000) + W_{r} = \dfrac{1}{2}(10^{-3})(5)^2$

$10 + W_{r} = 0.0125$

$W_{r} = 0.0125 - 10 = -9.9875$ J

$W_{r} \approx -9.98$ J

Answer: $-9.98$

subject: Physics
q: An artillery piece of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> fires a shell of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> horizontally. Instantaneously after the firing, the ratio of kinetic energy of the artillery and that of the shell is :
question: An artillery piece of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> fires a shell of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> horizontally. Instantaneously after the firing, the ratio of kinetic energy of the artillery and that of the shell is :
solution:
As linear momentum will be conserved, p⇀1=p⇀2

Now, kinetic energy can be written as KE=p22M.

Also p is the same for both masses. We can write,

KE∝1m

⇒KE1KE2=p22M1p22M2=M2M1

subject: Physics
q: A stone of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>, tied to a string is being whirled in a vertical circle with a uniform speed. The tension in the string is
question: A stone of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>, tied to a string is being whirled in a vertical circle with a uniform speed. The tension in the string is
options: minimum when the rope is in the horizontal position.
minimum at the highest position of the circular path.
minimum at the lowest position of the circular path.
solution:
As the speed is constant, centripetal force required towards the centre of the circle will be same. At the top, direction of gravitational force and tension acting on the object will be same. Therefore, we can write,

mg+T=mv2r ⇒T=mv2r-mg.

 Clearly in this case, tension in the string will be minimum.

subject: Physics
q: A sand dropper drops sand of mass $m(t)$ on a conveyer belt at a rate proportional to the square root of speed $(v)$ of the belt, i.e. $\frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \sqrt{v}$. If P is the power delivered to run the belt at constant speed then which of the following relationship is true?
question: A sand dropper drops sand of mass $m(t)$ on a conveyer belt at a rate proportional to the square root of speed $(v)$ of the belt, i.e. $\frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \sqrt{v}$. If P is the power delivered to run the belt at constant speed then which of the following relationship is true?
solution:
Power $=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{V}$
and $F=\frac{d p}{d t}=$ Rate of change of linear momentum
$F=V \cdot \frac{d m}{d t}=K_1 V^{\frac{3}{2}}, K$ is constant
Power $(P)=\left(K V^{\frac{3}{2}}\right) \cdot(V)$
$=K V^{\frac{5}{2}}$

So, $P^2 \propto V^5$

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass m, lying on a smooth horizontal surface, is attached to a spring (of negligible mass) of spring constant k. The other end of the spring is fixed, as shown in the figure. The block is initially at rest in its equilibrium position. If now the block is pulled with a constant force F, the maximum speed of the block is:
question: A block of mass m, lying on a smooth horizontal surface, is attached to a spring (of negligible mass) of spring constant k. The other end of the spring is fixed, as shown in the figure. The block is initially at rest in its equilibrium position. If now the block is pulled with a constant force F, the maximum speed of the block is:
solution:
When block attains maximum speed net force on m is zero. ∴F=kx

Or x=Fk

From work energy theorem

-12 k Fk2+FFk=12mVmax2

F22k=12mVmax2

Vmax=Fkm

subject: Physics
q:
Water falls from a 40 m high dam at the rate of 9×104 kg per hour. Fifty percentage of gravitational potential energy can be converted into electrical energy. Using this hydro electric energy number of 100 W lamps, that can be lit, is
(Take g=10 ms-2)
question:
Water falls from a 40 m high dam at the rate of 9×104 kg per hour. Fifty percentage of gravitational potential energy can be converted into electrical energy. Using this hydro electric energy number of 100 W lamps, that can be lit, is
(Take g=10 ms-2)
solution:
Since 50% efficiency is given, half of the energy of the falling water will be used by the bulbs. Therefore,

12∆mghΔt=n×P⇒12×9×1043600×10×40=100n⇒n=50

subject: Physics
q: An engine is attached to a wagon through a shock absorber of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The system with a total mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> when the brakes are applied to bring it to rest. In the process of the system being brought to rest, the spring of the shock absorber gets compressed by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of energy of the wagon is lost due to friction, the spring constant is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
question: An engine is attached to a wagon through a shock absorber of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The system with a total mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> when the brakes are applied to bring it to rest. In the process of the system being brought to rest, the spring of the shock absorber gets compressed by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of energy of the wagon is lost due to friction, the spring constant is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Work =ΔK.E.

Wtriction +Wspring =0-12mv2

-9010012mv2+Wspring =-12mv2

wspring =-10100×12mv2

-12kx2=-120mv2⇒k=40000×(20)210×(1)2=16×105

subject: Physics
q: A small particle of mass m moves in such a way that its potential energy U=12mω2r2 where ω is constant and r is the distance of the particle from origin. Assuming Bohr’s quantization of momentum and circular orbit, the radius of nth orbit will be proportional to
question: A small particle of mass m moves in such a way that its potential energy U=12mω2r2 where ω is constant and r is the distance of the particle from origin. Assuming Bohr’s quantization of momentum and circular orbit, the radius of nth orbit will be proportional to
solution:
The data given is

U=12mω2r2

From Bohr's quantization, 

mvr=nh2π⇒v2=nh2πrm2   ...(i)

In an orbit the value of the kinetic energy is half of the potential energy,

K=mω2r24

Substituting the value of equation (i) in the kinetic energy,

K=12mn2h24π2m2r2=mω2r24⇒n2h22π2m2ω2=r4⇒r∝n

subject: Physics
q: A person pushes a box on a rough horizontal plateform surface. He applies a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>N</mi></math> over a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math>. Thereafter, he gets progressively tired and his applied force reduces linearly with distance to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>N</mi></math>. The total distance through which the box has been moved is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math>. What is the work done by the person during the total movement of the box?
question: A person pushes a box on a rough horizontal plateform surface. He applies a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>N</mi></math> over a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math>. Thereafter, he gets progressively tired and his applied force reduces linearly with distance to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>N</mi></math>. The total distance through which the box has been moved is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math>. What is the work done by the person during the total movement of the box?
solution:
W=area=(200×15)+12(100+200)×15

=3000+2250

W=5250 J

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass $2$ kg begins to move under the influence of time dependent force $\vec{F} = (2t\hat{i} + 6t^2\hat{j})$ N, where $\hat{i}$ and $\hat{j}$ are unit vectors along $x$ and $y$-axis respectively. The power produced by the force at $t = 2$ s is _____ W.
question: A body of mass $2$ kg begins to move under the influence of time dependent force $\vec{F} = (2t\hat{i} + 6t^2\hat{j})$ N, where $\hat{i}$ and $\hat{j}$ are unit vectors along $x$ and $y$-axis respectively. The power produced by the force at $t = 2$ s is _____ W.
solution:
Given $\vec{F} = (2t\hat{i} + 6t^2\hat{j})$ N and $m = 2$ kg.

Acceleration of the body is $\vec{a} = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{m} = \dfrac{2t\hat{i} + 6t^2\hat{j}}{2} = t\hat{i} + 3t^2\hat{j}$ m/s$^2$.

Since the body begins to move from rest, its velocity at time $t$ is given by $\vec{v} = \int \vec{a} dt = \int (t\hat{i} + 3t^2\hat{j}) dt = \dfrac{t^2}{2}\hat{i} + t^3\hat{j}$ m/s.

At $t = 2$ s, the force is $\vec{F} = 2(2)\hat{i} + 6(2)^2\hat{j} = 4\hat{i} + 24\hat{j}$ N.

At $t = 2$ s, the velocity is $\vec{v} = \dfrac{(2)^2}{2}\hat{i} + (2)^3\hat{j} = 2\hat{i} + 8\hat{j}$ m/s.

Power produced by the force is $P = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{v} = (4\hat{i} + 24\hat{j}) \cdot (2\hat{i} + 8\hat{j})$.

$P = (4 \times 2) + (24 \times 8) = 8 + 192 = 200$ W.

Answer: $200$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: An object moves from position $r_{1}$ to position $r_{2}$ under a conservative force field $\vec{F}$. The work done by the force is $W=-\int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} \vec{F} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}$.
Statement II: Any object moving from one location to another location can follow infinite number of paths. Therefore, the amount of work done by the object changes with the path it follows for a conservative force.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: An object moves from position $r_{1}$ to position $r_{2}$ under a conservative force field $\vec{F}$. The work done by the force is $W=-\int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} \vec{F} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}$.
Statement II: Any object moving from one location to another location can follow infinite number of paths. Therefore, the amount of work done by the object changes with the path it follows for a conservative force.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
Statement I:
The work done by a force $\vec{F}$ during a displacement from $\vec{r}_1$ to $\vec{r}_2$ is defined as $W = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{r}$.
The expression given in the statement, $W = -\int_{r_1}^{r_2} \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{r}$, is the definition of the change in potential energy ($\Delta U = -W$), not the work done by the force itself.
Thus, Statement I is false.

Statement II:
By definition, a conservative force is one for which the work done in moving an object between two points is independent of the path taken.
It only depends on the initial and final positions.
Therefore, the claim that work done changes with the path for a conservative force is false.

Since both statements are false, the correct option is (1).

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving with a momentum of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Now a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> acts on the body in the direction of its motion for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The increase in the Kinetic energy of the body is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving with a momentum of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Now a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> acts on the body in the direction of its motion for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The increase in the Kinetic energy of the body is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
The formula to calculate the change on momentum of the object is given by

pf-pi=∫Fdt   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the final momentum of the object.

pf-10 kg m s-1=2 N×5 s⇒pf=10+10 kg m s-1= 20 kg m s-1

The formula to calculate increase in kinetic energy ∆K of the particle is given by

∆K=12mpf2-pi2   ...2

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (2) to calculate the required increase in kinetic energy.

∆K=12×5 kg×202-102 kg2 m2 s-2= 30 J
